coredata.dc:title: Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots with single wavelength excitation for white light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Kangxu
Zheng, Xuan
Zhang, Huijun
Xu, Menghan
Wang, Shan
Yang, Quanling
Xiong, Chuanxi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been applied in numerous fields by virtue of their excellent features. Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots with high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield are still the emphases for research. Here, by using 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid and 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid, multi-color-red, green, blue (RGB) emitting CDs (RGB-CDs) were successfully synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal route. The RGB-CDs exhibited stable optical properties and significant photoluminescence characteristics with excitation-independent. Structure and element analysis indicated that conjugated graphite structure of carbon cores and oxygen on surface states were responsible for the RGB color emissions of carbon dots. Moreover, RGB-color and white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) were fabricated by these multi-color fluorescent carbon dots. The LEDs exhibited excellent lighting performance, indicating that these RGB-CDs have the potential to substitute rare elements for solid-state optoelectronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solvothermal
Photoluminescence
Excitation-independent
Optoelectronic applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the RGB-CDs/PDMS composite films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a-c) The UV-absorption spectra, PL excitation and PL emission spectra of three samples.

a-c) TEM images of a) B-CDs, b) G-CDs and c) R-CDs, the insets show particle diameter distributions. d-i) High-resolution XPS C1s and O1s spectra of B-CDs (d, g), G-CDs (e, h) and R-CDs (f, i), respectively.
a) FT-IR spectra of the RGB-CDs. b) Energy gap structures to explain the mechanism of RGB-CDs with different emissions.

a) The production and photographs of RGB-CDs/PDMS composite films under 365 nm excitation light. b-d) The optical images of blue, green, and red LED devices and the EL emission spectrum of working LED devices. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

a) The CIE 1931 containing color coordinates of RGB-CDs-LED devices. b) The EL emission spectrum of WLED. Insets show optical image of WLED (left) and white PL emission spectrum and photographs of RGB-CDs solution under 365 nm UV light (right). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

a) The solvothermal synthetic route for preparation of blue, green, and red emitting carbon dots. b) The photographs of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs dispersed in ethanol in daylight (left), and under excitation of 365 nm (right).

head.ce:title: Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots with single wavelength excitation for white light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been applied in numerous fields by virtue of their excellent features. Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots with high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield are still the emphases for research. Here, by using 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid and 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid, multi-color-red, green, blue (RGB) emitting CDs (RGB-CDs) were successfully synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal route. The RGB-CDs exhibited stable optical properties and significant photoluminescence characteristics with excitation-independent. Structure and element analysis indicated that conjugated graphite structure of carbon cores and oxygen on surface states were responsible for the RGB color emissions of carbon dots. Moreover, RGB-color and white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) were fabricated by these multi-color fluorescent carbon dots. The LEDs exhibited excellent lighting performance, indicating that these RGB-CDs have the potential to substitute rare elements for solid-state optoelectronic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are one type of novel carbon materials after graphene and carbon nanotubes. Since CDs were discovered in 2004 [1], researchers have made great progress in expanding their applications such as solar cells [2–4], biological application [5–7], sensing detection [8–11], and supercapacitor [12,13]. Recently, to realize intelligent electronic display systems, there is an increasing demand to facilitate functional features of carbon dots in optoelectronic devices [14–19]. For instance, tunable PL emission, modified surface structure, doped chemical element and solid-liquid photoluminescence, are effective measures of applying CDs in the optoelectronic devices [20–23]. Compared to novel photoelectric materials like perovskites and some inorganic quantum dots (ZnSe, CdSe, CuInS2), carbon dots have the advantages of green initiative, stability and low cost [24]. In order to obtain the superiority of CDs, efforts should be concentrated on designing and synthesizing multi-color fluorescent CDs.

Trichromatic luminescence as well as high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) has now become the basic unit of full color display. However, most CDs prepared by conventional approaches emit blue or green color and exhibit low photoluminescence quantum yield in general. Compared with the primary short fluorescence emission wavelength in conventional methods, more favorable candidates for preparation of CDs have been considered to overcome those limitations by controlling reaction conditions to affect their sizes, graphitic nitrogen contents and surface groups [25–30]. For example, Lin's group reported three RGB PL-color CDs and proposed that the differences in particle size and nitrogen content result in red-shift emission [30]. Sun's group found that the emission of CDs can be turned from 430 to 630 nm by controlling graphitization and surface functional groups [25]. Similarly, Ding et al. obtained a series of CDs with emission varying from 443 to 745 nm through changing reaction solvents, which govern dehydration and carbonization processes [31]. Obviously, CDs have the opportunity for optoelectronic applications with great progress of the multi-color and high PLQY. But it's also important to mention that only a few studies have demonstrated long-wavelength emission carbon dots for LED devices, and the synthetic routes for multiple-color-emissive CDs have not been well achieved in application for LED devices. Hence, there are still significant challenges in discovering liable and mild approaches to prepare multi-color CDs.

In this work, we developed a succinct approach to synthesize three-primary-color florescent emission CDs with high photoluminescence quantum yields. The more sp2 conjugate region and oxygen functional groups lead to narrower energy gap, resulting in a significant red shift of photoluminescence in our case. The precursors of diaminobenzoic acid contain polymerizable functional groups and maintain conjugated structure after dehydration reaction which is conducive to regulating the fluorescence of carbon dots. No requiring the complicated and varied precursors and solvents, Blue-CDs (B-CDs) and Green-CDs (G-CDs) were obtained under the solvothermal treatment of 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid and Red-CDs (R-CDs) were prepared from 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid by a similar process. Considering the category and cost of precursors is quite simple and feasible in our case, it is of great significance to further promote the optoelectronic applications of CDs as well as bulk synthesis. The three CDs exhibited excellent and stable photoluminescence both in solution and solid matrices. Futhermore, RGB-color LEDs were fabricated by covering RGB emitting CDs/Silica gel composites on 365 nm emissive InGaN chips, and the mixed RGB-CDs drived white fluorescence for white LEDs (WLEDs) with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage 1931 (CIE 1931) of (0.2963, 0.3225) and CCT of 7452 K. Due to excellent fluorescent performance and the easiness of preparation, they could be further developed and used for constructing flexible organic light-emitting diode (OLED) displays.

3,5-diaminobenzoic acid, 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid were purchased from Energy Chemical. Methanol, PDMS, ethanol, dichloromethane and phosphoric acid were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Quinine sulfate, Rhodamine 6G and Rhodamine B were purchased from Aladdin. Transparent silicone resin (A gel, B gel) was purchased from Looking Long (Shenzhen) Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

The RGB-CDs were prepared by one-pot solvothermal process. Typically, 0.1 g 3,5-DABA (3,5-diaminobenzoic acid) and 3 mL phosphoric acid were dissolved in 12 mL ethanol for the synthesis of green emissive CDs. The solvents were transferred into 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 8 h. The blue emissive CDs were prepared by the same method without adding phosphoric acid.

The R-CDs were synthesized by 0.4 g 3,4-DABA (3,4-diaminobenzoic acid), which was dissolved in 12 mL ethanol and transferred into 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 12 h, as shown in Scheme 1 . After solvothermal treatment, the autoclaves were cooled to room temperature and then neutralized phosphoric acid with sodium hydroxide solution. Afterwards, the mixed solution was centrifuged at 11000 r/min for 20 min to remove the large sheet. The supernatant was collected and thin−layer chromatography (TLC) was applied to the separation of objective CDs in the supernatants as a result of different polarities. Specifically, the supernatant was pipetted with a plastic dropper 3 mL, scribbled in a straight line on a TLC plate (200*200 mm), and putted in chromatography developing cylinder filled with 20 mL of methanol and dichloromethane with a volume ratio of 1:10 as developing agent. Finally, Silicone powder absorbing carbon dots was scraped off and dispersed in ethanol, followed by filtration with a syringe filter (0.22 μm). Consequently, three kinds of florescent emission CDs (B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs) were successfully collected and then dispersed in ethanol (Scheme 1, left). The aforementioned RGB-CDs ethanol solutions also exhibited bright PL emissions for blue, green and red color respectively, under a single-wavelength UV light (Scheme 1, right).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the RGB-CDs/PDMS composite films
ce:section.ce:para: In order to distribute the carbon dots, a certain amount of RGB-CDs solutions were mixed in PDMS, The mixture was slowly stirred to form a uniform mixture. Then mixed with hardener in 10:1 mass ratio after ethanol evaporated. The mixture was poured into a mold and left for two hours at room temperature for curing to achieve the RGB-CDs/PDMS composites. Typically, 1 mL B-CDs (0.5 wt %) solution was dissolved in 3 mL PDMS. 300 μL hardener was then added into the mixture with stirring. After 2 h curing, the blue emissive B-CDs/PDMS composite film and majuscule were obtained.

The RGB-CDs/Silica gel was drop-casted on the 365 nm emissive InGaN chip (the voltage of 3.2 V) carefully, and cured at 120 °C for 3 h. In detail, 5 μg B-CDs were mixed with 200 μL A gel and B gel with 1:4 mass ratio, and then the mixture was drop-casted on the chip for the blue LED. Similar steps were done for the green and red LEDs. For WLEDs, 1 mL B-CDs, 500 μL G-CDs and 1.5 mL R-CDs were dissolved in ethanol for white fluorescence emission, and then mixed with A gel and B gel to fabricate LEDs.

The UV–visible absorption spectra were measured using Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were measured on an RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer with the slit width of 3 nm. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of RGB-CDs were obtained by a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope. The software analysis of the TEM image was used to estimate particle diameter distribution of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded by ESCALAB 250Xi. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected on PerkinElmer spectrometer. Raman spectra were obtained on LABRAN HR Evolution by excitation of 633 nm. All the above experiments were completed at room temperature.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the RGB-CDs are shown in Fig. 1 a–c. Typical π-π* transitions in the region of 200–280 nm are observed, corresponding to the π-π* transitions of CC bonds, whilst the absorption peaks at 320 nm are originated from n-π* transition of CO and CN [32,33]. In addition, lower energy absorption bands at 400, 450 and 550 nm respectively, indicate different surface states of the RGB-CDs [34,35]. The photoluminescence (PL) emission of B-CDs was at 450 nm with excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. 1a). The PL peak of G-CDs was at 500 nm under excitation of 440 nm (Fig. 1b). The emission peak of R-CDs located at 600 nm with maximum excitation wavelength at 580 nm (Fig. 1c). As shown in Fig. S1, the emission peaks of RGB-CDs peaks didn't change with the excitation wavelength shifting, indicating that the RGB-CDs demonstrated significant excitation-independent emissions. The relative PL QYs for B-, G- and R-CDs were calculated to be 29.2%, 68.4% and 5% (Table S1) respectively, using the PL intensity gradients against absorption (Fig. S2). Moreover, the relative QYs of G-CDs and R-CDs were measured under the optimal excitation wavelengths for 460 nm and 580 nm, as shown in Table S2. The relative QYs reached 69.2% and 24.8%, respectively, revealing outstanding fluorescence and excitation independent characteristics. With regard to their various PL emissions, the structure and elemental characterization were complemented for further analysis.

The morphologies of RGB-CDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to understand the individual color results. Derived from Fig. 2 a–c, the TEM images demonstrate that the well-dispersed particles are spherical in shape and the average sizes are 2.05, 2.65 and 3.1 nm for the blue CDs, green CDs, and red CDs, respectively. According to the average sizes of these CDs, the PL emission red shift from B-CDs to R-CDs cannot be obviously attributed to differences in particle size or the quantum size effects (QSE).

Raman spectroscopy was obtained to further inquire the chemical bond information of RGB-CDs. As shown in Fig. S2, the Raman spectra present two evident peaks of graphite carbon at 1358 cm−1 for the disordered (D band) and 1582 cm−1 for the graphite (G band), respectively [36]. The intensity ratios of D and G bands (ID/IG) are 1.43, 1.14 and 1.06 for B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. The decreasing ID/IG ratios is most likely associated with the reaction condition influencing graphitization of the carbon core. Based on continuous carbonation reaction, the incremental extent of graphitization is closely connected with the red shift of PL emission in RGB-CDs [25,26].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to study the functional groups of the RGB-CDs. The evaluation results show that the three samples demonstrate similar chemical compositions and three major elements (C1s 284.7 eV, N1s 398.8 eV and O1s 532.4 eV) are found in the nanomaterials (Fig. S4). In the highersolution XPS spectra (Fig. 2d–i), the resolved C1s spectra at 284.4, 285.6, 286.6 and 287.7 eV indicate the presence of CC/CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively. The sp2 carbon (CC/CC) contents of RGB-CDs gradually increase from 69.9% (B-CDs) to 73.9% (R-CDs), which implies more graphitization structure in agreement with the Raman results. In O1s curves, the deconvoluted peaks correspond to CO (531.5 eV) and CO (533.1 eV) bands, respectively. In addition, the atomic ratios of oxygen varied from 12% (B-CDs) to 22.2% (R-CDs) and the O/C atomic ratios for R-CDs and G-CDs (30.1% and 29.8%) are higher than B-CDs (17.2%) (Table S3), suggesting an incremental oxygen functional groups in these CDs. For N1s, the high resolution N1s spectra (Fig. S4 d-f) show the decrease in relative amount of pyridinic N (398.3 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.5 eV). On the contrary, the relative amount of graphitic N (400.2 eV) gradually increase for B-, G- and R-CDs, similar to previous reports [28,31].

To gain further insight into the chemical structure of the carbon dots, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 3 a) were studied and revealed the presence of abundant functional groups, such as OH (3440 cm−1), NH (3360 cm−1), CO (1692 cm−1) and CO (1260 cm−1). Obviously, the OH vibration bands of G-CDs and R-CDs are broader and flatter than B-CDs, indicating diversiform structures of oxygen-containing groups are on the nanoparticle surfaces of G-CDs and R-CDs compared with B-CDs [29,37]. In addition, the relative intensity of stretching vibration intensity for CO at 1677 cm−1 and CO at 1260 cm−1 gradually rise for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, illustrating that the three CDs contain distinctive degree of oxidation. In combination with O/C atomic ratios from XPS data, the oxygen functional groups on the surfaces of RGB-CDs play a part in inducing a red shift of PL [25,29,38,39].

In current reports, it is verified that increasing size of CDs as well as assigning different functional groups give rise to the lower energy gap between the HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) and LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) energy levels, and then causing the red shift of PL emission [16,23,40,41]. The influence of graphite content on the size of the gap has been widely investigated [42–44]. In general, an increase in the size of sp2 clusters or conjugation length causes a decrease in energy gap [27,45–47]. In addition, the surface chemical status is also a major factor in present reports [25,48]. Yan et al. have studied oxidation functional groups on sp2 carbons can induce significant local distortion and then create new energy gaps [49]. Similarly, the much larger hybridization of CO with LUMO than HOMO leads to the narrower energy gap [40]. Regarding the size-dependent emission is a unique feature of CDs, in contrast to conventional size-fluorescent CDs, our resultant CDs exhibit uniform average sizes of 2–3 nm, and the R-CDs show higher degree of sp2 region than others from Raman spectroscopy as well as larger oxygen groups from XPS and FTIR analyses. Based on the above results, we speculate that the PL emission from blue to red is related to the content of conjugated-carbon and oxygen (Fig. 3b), and the variation in conjugated sites and more oxygen functional groups of RGB-CDs give rise to the red shift emission in our project.

Furthermore, as the result of rich functional groups, RGB-CDs have good solubility in many organic solvents such as acetone, dichloromethane, DMF, and methanol, which can be applied in a variety of areas. Considering the solid-state fluorescence stability of carbon dots, we manufactured RGB-CDs/PDMS composites as PDMS is an optically transparent and easily-used polymer. In detail, RGB-CDs were dissolved in ethanol to form uniform solutions, then the monomer of PDMS was added and stirred evenly. The mixtures were mixed with hardener in 10:1 mass ratio after ethanol evaporated. Finally, the prepared solutions were poured into molds and left two hours at room temperature for curing to achieve the RGB-CDs/PDMS composites. In Fig. 4 a, the RGB-CDs/PDMS composite films and the capital letters “W”, “U” and “T” emission colors remain stable for a long time which are same as those of the original RGB-CDs under UV light of 365 nm, illustrating steady fluorescence of the resultant CDs.

The excitation-independent behavior of RGB-CDs in solid polymer matrix indicate that the as-prepared CDs can be used as phosphor for light-emitting diodes. To fabricate down-conversion light-emitting diodes, the RGB-CDs/Silica gel composites were coated on a 365 nm excitation light emitting chip (the details can be found in Experimental Section). Fig. 4b–d show the EL spectra of RGB-LEDs under the applied voltage of 3.2 V. The maximum emission peaks of blue, green, and red emissive CDs-based LEDs are 448, 515 and 618 nm which are closely related to the spectral red shift. The ratios of blue, green, and red areas to respective EL spectra are 36.1%, 40.9%, and 42.7% as shown in Table S4 in the Supporting Information. The ratios indicate that the RGB-CDs based phosphors trigger different light-emitting wavelengths for LEDs. Additionally, the CIE 1931 coordinates of blue, green, and red LED devices locate at (0.1427, 0.0957), (0.1578, 0.4726) and (0.5356, 0.4064), respectively (Fig. 5 a). It has to be mentioned that the spectrum center of R-CDs based LED slants orange-red from CIE 1931 coordinates.

Based on the attractive red–green–blue tri-color emissions of the CDs in coordinates, white emissive CDs have been obtained. The white emissive CDs mixture was obtained through blending different CDs solutions which those concentration of CDs solutions are 0.5 wt %. The optimum ratio of white emissive mixture is 1 mL B-CDs, 500 μL G-CDs and 1.5 mL R-CDs (Fig. S5 and Table S5). The mixture emitted a cool white light under 365 nm ultra-violet excitation and the PL emission wavelength covered most of the visible-light area from 400 nm to 700 nm (Fig. 5b). The EL spectrum of white RGB-CDs-LEDs also shows a wide peak covering most of the visible-light region (Fig. 5b) and the CIE 1931 coordinates are CCT of 7452 K and (0.2963, 0.3225), which is in close proximity to pure white light (0.33, 0.33). Compared with some recently developed multi-color fluorescent carbon dots (Table S6), the RGB-CDs synthesized from DABA have significant benefits in preparation, quantum yield, and CIE. The above results further support that these stable RGB-CDs have the potential to apply in future optoelectronic devices.

In summary, we reported a new synthetic approach for red, green and blue color emitting CDs through simple solvothermal pyrolysis. The three kinds of CDs exhibited bright RGB luminescence and excitation independent property both in solution and solid states. The fluorescence quantum yield of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs reached 29.2%, 69.2% and 24.8%, respectively, under the optimal excitation wavelengths. The different PL emissions of the RGB-CDs resulted from the differences in sp2 carbon structure and more oxygen functional groups. Moreover, the RGB-CDs/PDMS mixtures displayed emission light of their original CDs, indicating the stable PL properties of RGB-CDs. In particular, we used RGB-CDs as efficient phosphor to fabricate RGB-CDs-based LED devices with maximum emission peaks at 448, 515 and 618 nm. Utilizing the white emission of the mixed RGB-CDs, the white LED devices were achieved with CIE 1931 coordinates of (0.2963, 0.3225) and CCT of 7452 K. This work provides new insights into the methods of innovation for the development of RGB emissive CDs and is beneficial for real application in display-based fields.
The authors declare that no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51673154, 51503159, and 51703177), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WUT: 2016-YB-011, 2018III009, 2018IVB022).
